title: Differentiate Everything with a Reversible Domain-Specific Language.

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## Differentiate Everything with a Reversible Embeded Domain-Specific Language

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## Abstract

Reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) suffers from the issue of having too much space overhead to trace back intermediate computational states for back-propagation. The traditional method to trace back states is called checkpointing that stores intermediate states into a global stack and restore state through either stack pop or re-computing. The overhead of stack manipulations and re-computing makes the general purposed (not tensor-based) AD engines unable to meet many industrial needs. Instead of checkpointing, we propose to use reverse computing to trace back states by designing and implementing a reversible programming eDSL, where a program can be executed bi-directionally without implicit stack operations. The absence of implicit stack operations makes the program compatible with existing compiler features, including utilizing existing optimization passes and compiling the code as GPU kernels. We implement AD for sparse matrix operations and some machine learning applications to show that our framework has the state-of-the-art performance.

## 1 Introduction

Most of the popular automatic differentiation (AD) tools in the market, such as TensorFlow (Abadi et al., 2015), Pytorch (Paszke et al., 2017), and Flux (Innes et al., 2018) implements reverse mode AD at the tensor level to meet the need in machine learning. Later, People in the scientific computing domain also realized the power of these AD tools, they use these tools to solve scientific problems such as seismic inversion (Zhu et al., 2020), variational quantum circuits simulation (Bergholm et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019) and variational tensor network simulation (Liao et al., 2019; Roberts et al., 2019). To meet the diverse need in these applications, one sometimes has to define backward rules manually, for example

1. To differentiate sparse matrix operations used in Hamiltonian engineering (Hao Xie & Wang), people defined backward rules for sparse matrix multiplication and dominant eigensolvers (Golub & Van Loan, 2012),

2. In tensor network algorithms to study the phase transition problem (Liao et al., 2019; Seeger et al., 2017; Wan & Zhang, 2019; Hubig, 2019), people defined backward rules for singular value decomposition (SVD) function and QR decomposition (Golub & Van Loan, 2012).

Instead of defining backward rules manually, one can also use a general purposed AD (GP-AD) framework like Tapenade (Hascoet & Pascual, 2013), OpenAD (Utke et al., 2008) and Zygote (Innes, 2018; Innes et al., 2019). Researchers have used these tools in practical applications such as bundle adjustment (Shen & Dai, 2018) and earth system simulation (Forget et al., 2015), where differentiating scalar operations is important. However, the power of these tools are often limited by their relatively poor performance. In many practical applications, a program might do billions of computations. In each computational step, the AD engine might cache some data for backpropagation. (Griewank & Walther, 2008) Frequent caching of data slows down the program significantly, while the memory usage will become a bottleneck as well. Caching implicitly also make these frameworks incompatible with kernel functions. To avoid such issues, we need a new GP-AD framework that does not cache automatically for users.
In this paper, we propose to implement the reverse mode AD on a reversible (domain-specific) programming language (Perumalla, 2013; Frank, 2017), where intermediate states can be traced

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

backward without accessing an implicit stack. Reversible programming allows people to utilize the reversibility to reverse a program. In machine learning, reversibility is proven to substantially decrease the memory usage in unitary recurrent neural networks (MacKay et al., 2018), normalizing flow (Dinh et al., 2014), hyper-parameter learning (Maclaurin et al., 2015) and residual neural networks (Gomez et al., 2017; Behrmann et al., 2018). Reversible programming will make these happen naturally. The power of reversible programming is not limited to handling these reversible applications, any program can be written in a reversible style. Converting an irreversible program to the reversible form would cost overheads in time and space. Reversible programming provides a flexible time-space trade-off scheme that different with checkpointing (Griewank, 1992; Griewank & Walther, 2008; Chen et al., 2016), _reverse computing_ (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990), to let user handle these overheads explicitly. There have been many prototypes of reversible languages like Janus (Lutz, 1986), R (not the popular one) (Frank, 1997), Erlang (Lanese et al., 2018) and object-oriented ROOPL (Haulund, 2017). In the past, the primary motivation to study reversible programming is to support reversible computing devices (Frank & Knight Jr, 1999) such as adiabatic complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) (Koller & Athas, 1992), molecular mechanical computing system (Merkle et al., 2018) and superconducting system (Likharev, 1977; Semenov et al., 2003; Takeuchi et al., 2014; 2017), and these reversible computing devices are orders more energy-efficient. Landauer proves that only when a device does not erase information (i.e. reversible), its energy efficiency can go beyond the thermal dynamic limit. (Landauer, 1961; Reeb & Wolf, 2014) However, these reversible programming languages can not be used directly in real scientific computing, since most of them do not have basic elements like floating point numbers, arrays, and complex numbers. This motivates us to build a new embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) in Julia (Bezanson et al., 2012; 2017) as a new playground of GP-AD.

In this paper, we first compare the time-space trade-off in the optimal checkpointing and the optimal reverse computing in Sec. 2. Then we introduce the language design of NiLang in Sec. 3. In Sec. 4, we explain the implementation of automatic differentiation in NiLang. In Sec. 5, we benchmark the performance of NiLang’s AD with other AD software and explain why it is fast.

## 2 Reverse computing as an Alternative to Checkpointing

One can use either checkpointing or reverse computing to trace back intermediate states of a _T_ - step computational process _s_ 1 = _f_ 1( _s_ 0), _s_ 2 = _f_ 2( _s_ 1), . . . , _sT_ = _fT_ ( _sT_ −1) with a run-time memory _S_ . In the checkpointing scheme, the program first takes snapshots of states at certain time steps _S_ = { _sa_ , _sb_ , . . .}, 1 ≤ _a_ < _b_ < ... ≤ _T_ by running a forward pass. When retrieving a state _sk_ , if _sk_ ∈ _S_ , just return this state, otherwise, return max _s j_ < _k_ ∈ _S_ and re-compute _sk_ from _s j_ . In the reverse _j_ computing scheme, one first writes the program in a reversible style. Without prior knowledge, a regular program can be transpiled to the reversible style is by doing the transformation in Listing. 1.

Listing 1: Transpiling a regular code to the reversible code without prior knowledge.

Listing 2: The reverse of Listing. 1

Then one can visit states in the reversed order by running the reversed program in Listing. 2, which erases the computed results from the tail. One may argue that easing through uncomputing is not necessary here. This is not true for a general reversible program, because the intermediate states might be mutable and used in other parts of the program. It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1). The checkpointing scheme snapshots the output in every step, and the reverse computing scheme allocates extra storage for storing outputs in every step. On the other side, only checkpointing can achieve a zero space overhead by recomputing everything from the beginning _s_ 0, with a time complexity _O_ ( _T_[2] ). The difference in space overheads can be explained by the difference of the optimal checkpointing and optimal reverse computing algorithms. The optimal checkpointing algorithm that widely used in AD is the treeverse algorithm in Fig. 1(a). This algorithm partitions the computational process **binomially** into _d_ sectors. At the beginning of each sector, the program snapshots the state and push it into a global stack, hence the memory for checkpointing is _dS_ . The states in the last sector are retrieved by the above space-efficient _O_ ( _T_[2] ) algorithm. After that, the last snapshot can be freed and the program has one more quota in memory. With the freed memory, the second last sector can be further partition into two sectors. Likewise, the _l_ th sectors is partitioned into _l_ sub-sectors, where _l_ is the sector index counting from the tail. Recursively apply this treeverse algorithm _t_ times until the sector size is 1. The approximated overhead in time and space are
where _T_ = η( _t_ , _d_ ) holds. By carefully choosing either a _t_ or _d_ , the overhead in time and space can be both logarithmic.

On the other side, the optimal time-space trade-off scheme in reverse computing is the Bennett’s algorithm illustrated in Fig. 1 (b). It evenly **evenly** partition the program into _k_ sectors. The program marches forward ( _P_ process) for _k_ steps to obtain the final state _sk_ +1, then backward ( _Q_ process) from the _k_ − 1th step to erase the states in between _s_ 1< _i_ < _k_ . This process is also called the _compute-copyuncompute_ process. Recursively apply the compute-copy-uncompute process for each sector until each _P_ / _Q_ process contains only one unit computation. the time and space complexities are

Here, the overhead in time is polynomial, which is worse than the treeverse algorithm. The treeverse like partition does not apply here because the first sweep to create initial checkpoints without introducing any space overheads is not possible in reversible computing. In Appendix B.2, we show how to implement a unitary matrix multiplication without introducing overheads in space and time. _Second_ , reverse computing does not allocate automatically for users, user can optimize the memory access patterns for their own devices like GPU. _Third_ , reverse computing is compatible with effective codes, so that it fits better with modern languages. In Appendix B.3, we show reversible thinking can lead the user to a constant memory, constant time implementation of chained multiplication algorithms.

## 3 Language design

NiLang is an embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) NiLang built on top of the host language Julia (Bezanson et al., 2012; 2017). Julia is a popular language for scientific programming and machine learning. We choose Julia mainly for speed. Julia is a language with high abstraction, however, its clever design of type inference and just in time compiling make it has a C like speed. Meanwhile, it has rich features for meta-programming. Its package for pattern matching MLStyle allows us to define an eDSL in less than 2000 lines. Comparing with a regular reversible programming language, NiLang features array operations, rich number systems including floating-point numbers, complex numbers, fixed-point numbers, and logarithmic numbers. It also implements the compute-copy-uncompute (Bennett, 1973) macro to increase code reusability. Besides the above reversible hardware compatible features, it also has some reversible hardware incompatible features to meet the practical needs. For example, it views the floating-point + and − operations as reversible. It also allows users to extend instruction sets and sometimes inserting external statements. These features are not compatible with future reversible hardware. NiLang’s compiling process, grammar and operational semantics are described in Appendix G. The source code is also available online, we will put a link here after the anonymous open review session. By the time of writing, the version of NiLang is v0.7.3.

## 3.1 Reversible functions and instructions

Mathematically, any irreversible mapping `y = f(args...)` can be trivially transformed to its reversible form `y += f(args...)` or `y` ⊻ `= f(args...)` (⊻ is the bit-wise `XOR` ), where `y` is a pre-emptied variable. But in numeric computing with finite precision, this is not always true. The reversibility of arithmetic instruction is closely related to the number system. For integer and fixed

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

point number system, `y += f(args...)` and `y -= f(args...)` are rigorously reversible. For logarithmic number system and tropical number system (Speyer & Sturmfels, 2009), `y *= f(args...)` and `y /= f(args...)` as reversible (not introducing the zero element). While for floating point numbers, none of the above operations are rigorously reversible. However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion. In Appendix F, we will show doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation. Other reversible operations includes `SWAP` , `ROT` , `NEG` et. al., and this instruction set is extensible. One can define a reversible multiplier in NiLang as in Listing. 4.

Listing 4: A reversible multiplier

```
julia>usingNiLang
julia>@ifunctionmultiplier(y!::Real,a::Real,b::Real)
y!+=a*b
end
julia>multiplier(2,3,5)
(17,3,5)
julia>(~multiplier)(17,3,5)
(2,3,5)
```

Macro `@i` generates two functions that are reversible to each other, `multiplier` and ∼ `multiplier` , each defines a mapping R[3] → R[3] . The ! after a symbol is a part of the name, as a conversion to indicate the mutated variables.

## 3.2 Reversible memory management

A distinct feature of reversible memory management is that the content of a variable must be known when it is deallocated. We denote the allocation of a pre-emptied memory as `x` ← `0` , and its inverse, deallocating a **zero emptied** variable, as `x` → `0` . An unknown variable can not be deallocate, but can be pushed to a stack pop out later in the uncomputing stage. If a variable is allocated and deallocated in the local scope, we call it an ancilla. Listing. 5 defines the complex valued accumulative log function.

Here, the macro `@inline` tells the compiler that this function can be inlined. One can input “←” and “→” by typing “\leftarrow[TAB KEY]” and “\rightarrow[TAB KEY]” respectively in a Julia editor or REPL. NiLang does not have immutable structs, so that the real part `y!.re` and imaginary `y!.im` of a complex number can be changed directly. It is easy to verify that the bottom two lines in the function body are the inverse of the top two lines. i.e., the bottom two lines _uncomputes_ the top two lines. The motivation of uncomputing is to zero clear the contents in ancilla `n` so that it can be deallocated correctly. _Compute-copy-uncompute_ is a useful design pattern in reversible programming so that we created a pair of macros `@routine` and ∼ `@routine` for it. - 3.3 Reversible control flows

One can define reversible `if` , `for` and `while` statements in a reversible program. Fig. 2 (a) shows the flow chart of executing the reversible `if` statement. There are two condition expressions in this chart, a precondition and a postcondition. The precondition decides which branch to enter in the forward execution, while the postcondition decides which branch to enter in the backward execution. The pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes are shown in Listing. 7 and Listing. 8.

Fig. 2 (b) shows the flow chart of the reversible `while` statement. It also has two condition expressions. Before executing the condition expressions, the program presumes the postcondition is false. After each iteration, the program asserts the postcondition to be true. To reverse this statement, one can exchange the precondition and postcondition, and reverse the body statements. The pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes are shown in Listing. 9 and Listing. 10.

The reversible `for` statement is similar to the irreversible one except that after execution, the program will assert the iterator to be unchanged. To reverse this statement, one can exchange `start` and `stop` and inverse the sign of `step` . Listing. 11 computes the Fibonacci number recursively and reversibly.

`@i function rrfib(out!, n) @invcheckoff if (n >= 1, ~) counter` ← `0 counter += n while (counter > 1, counter!=n) rrfib(out!, counter-1) counter -= 2 end counter -= n % 2 counter` → `0 end out! += 1 end`

Here, `out!` is an integer initialized to `0` for storing outputs. The precondition and postcondition are wrapped into a tuple. In the `if` statement, the postcondition is the same as the precondition, hence we omit the postcondition by inserting a "∼" in the second field for “copying the precondition in this field as the postcondition”. In the while statement, the postcondition is true only for the initial loop. Once code is proven correct, one can turn off the reversibility check by adding `@invcheckoff` before a statement. This will remove the reversibility check and make the code faster and compatible with GPU kernels (kernel functions can not handle exceptions).

## 4 Reversible automatic differentiation

- 4.1 Back propagation

We decompose the problem of reverse mode AD into two sub-problems, **reversing the code** and **computing** ∂[multiple outputs]∂[single input][.][Reversing the code is trivial in reversible programming.][Computing the] gradient here is similar to forward mode automatic differentiation that computes[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]][.] Inspired by the Julia package ForwardDiff (Revels et al., 2016), we use the operator overloading technique to differentiate the program efficiently. In the backward pass, we wrap each output variable with a composite type `GVar` that containing an extra gradient field, and feed it into the reversed generic program. Instructions are multiple dispatched to corresponding gradient instructions that update the gradient field of `GVar` at the meantime of uncomputing. By reversing this gradient program, we can obtain the gradient program for the reversed program too. One can define the adjoint (“adjoint” here means the program for back-propagating gradients) of a primitive instruction as a reversible function on **either** the function itself or its reverse since the adjoint of a function’s reverse is equivalent to the reverse of the function’s adjoint.
It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗⃗ _yx_[=][ 1][.]

The implementation details are described in Appendix C. In most languages, operator overloading is accompanied with significant overheads of function calls and object allocation and deallocation. But in a language with type inference and just in time compiling like Julia, the boundary between two approaches are vague. The compiler inlines small functions, packs an array of constant sized immutable objects into a continuous memory, and truncates unnecessary branches automatically.

## 4.2 Hessians

Combining forward mode AD and reverse mode AD is a simple yet efficient way to obtain Hessians. By wrapping the elementary type with `Dual` defined in package ForwardDiff and throwing it into the gradient program defined in NiLang, one obtains one row/column of the Hessian matrix. We will use this approach to compute Hessians in the graph embedding benchmark in Sec. D.2.

## 4.3 CUDA kernels

CUDA programming is playing a significant role in high-performance computing. In Julia, one can write GPU compatible functions in native Julia language with KernelAbstractions. (Besard et al., 2017) Since NiLang does not push variables into stack automatically for users, it is safe to write differentiable GPU kernels with NiLang. We will differentiate CUDA kernels with no more than extra 10 lines in the bundle adjustment benchmark in Sec. 5.

## 5 Benchmarks

We benchmark our framework with the state-of-the-art GP-AD frameworks, including source code transformation based Tapenade and Zygote and operator overloading based ForwardDiff and ReverseDiff. Since most tensor based AD software like famous TensorFlow and PyTorch are not designed for the using cases used in our benchmarks, we do not include those package to avoid an unfair comparison. In the following benchmarks, the CPU device is Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6230 CPU @ 2.10GHz, and the GPU device is NVIDIA Titan V. For NiLang benchmarks, we have turned the reversibility check off to achieve a better performance.

We reproduced the benchmarks for Gaussian mixture model (GMM) and bundle adjustment in Srajer et al. (2018) by re-writing the programs in a reversible style. We show the results in Fig. 3. The Tapenade data is obtained by executing the docker file provided by the original benchmark, which provides a baseline for comparison.

## 6 Discussion

In this work, we demonstrate a new approach to back propagates a program called reverse computing AD by designing a reversible eDSL NiLang. NiLang is a powerful tool to differentiate code from the source code level so that can be directly useful to machine learning researches. It can generate efficient backward rules, which is exemplified in Appendix E. It can also be used to differentiate reversible neural networks like normalizing flows (Kobyzev et al., 2019) to save memory, e.g. backpropagating NICE network (Dinh et al., 2014) with only constant space overheads. NiLang is most useful in solving large scale scientific problems memory efficiently. In Liu et al. (2020), people solve the ground state problem of a 28 × 28 square lattice spin-glass by re-writing the quantum simulator with NiLang. There are some challenges in reverse computing AD too.
- The native BLAS and convolution operations in NiLang are not optimized for the memory layout, and are too slow comparing with state of the art machine learning libraries. We

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- need a better implementation of these functions in the reversible programming context so that it can be more useful in training traditional deep neural networks.
- Although we show some examples of training neural networks on GPU, the shared-reading of a variable is not allowed.

- NiLang’s IR does not have variable analysis. The uncomputing pass is not always necessary for the irreversible host language to deallocate memory. In many cases, the host language’s variable analysis can figure this out, but it is not guarantted.

Another interesting issue is how to make use of reversible computing devices to save energy in machine learning. Reversible computing is not always more energy efficient than irreversible computing. In the time-space trade-off scheme in Sec. 2, we show the time to uncompute a unit of memory is exponential to _n_ as _Qn_ = (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ , and the computing energy also increases exponentially. On the other side, the amount of energy to erase a unit of memory is a constant. When (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ > 1/ξ, erasing the memory irreversibly is more energy-efficient, where ξ is the energy ratio between a reversible operation (an instruction or a gate) and its irreversible counterpart.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents an ambitious approach to automatic differentiation using reversible computing through an embedded DSL in Julia. The core idea—replacing checkpointing with reversible execution to avoid memory overheads in reverse-mode AD—is conceptually compelling, especially for memory-constrained scientific computing applications. The implementation in NiLang demonstrates nontrivial engineering, particularly in handling reversible control flow and memory management, and the benchmarks show promising speedups on specific tasks like bundle adjustment.

Overview issues:

- Lack of comparison with modern AD systems on standard ML models: The paper omits comparisons with widely used AD frameworks such as PyTorch, JAX, or even Zygote on standard deep learning architectures (e.g., CNNs, Transformers). While the authors justify this by stating these tools are "not designed for the using cases" in their benchmarks, this回避s the central question of whether the proposed method generalizes beyond niche scientific computing problems. Without evaluating on models where memory and compute patterns resemble real-world ML workloads (e.g., with large tensor operations, dropout, or batch normalization), the practical significance of the approach remains unclear. Readers cannot assess whether the performance gains in bundle adjustment translate to broader applicability.

- Insufficient evidence for memory efficiency claims in complex programs: Although the paper claims reduced memory usage due to the absence of checkpointing stacks, it provides only analytical estimates and one memory plot (Fig. 4) for a relatively simple GMM model. There is no measurement of peak memory during backpropagation for larger or more complex programs, nor a comparison with checkpointing-based AD systems under equivalent conditions. The claim that reversible execution avoids memory bottlenecks is therefore not empirically substantiated. Moreover, the analysis in Eq. (6) includes logarithmic terms from Bennett's algorithm, yet the benchmarks appear to assume constant memory—this discrepancy between theoretical complexity and practical results needs clarification.

- Unrealistic assumption of full reversibility in floating-point arithmetic: The paper treats floating-point addition and subtraction as reversible (Sec. 3.1), dismissing round-off errors as negligible. However, in practice, floating-point operations are not invertible due to precision loss, and uncomputing via reverse operations will accumulate numerical errors, especially in deep computational chains. This undermines the correctness of gradient computations. While Appendix F is cited as justification, its absence prevents verification. Without rigorous testing of gradient accuracy—e.g., against finite differences or other AD tools—the numerical stability of the method remains in doubt, particularly for higher-order derivatives used in Hessian computations.

- Missing evaluation of higher-order derivative correctness and performance: The paper claims the framework can compute Hessians (Sec. 4.2) by combining forward and reverse modes, yet provides no quantitative results on Hessian accuracy or computational cost. The reference to "Sec. D.2" for graph embedding benchmarks is not fulfilled in the provided text. Given that Hessian computation is sensitive to both numerical stability and implementation correctness, this omission is significant. A credible demonstration would include error norms relative to baseline methods and timing data, especially since nested AD often exposes inefficiencies in AD system designs.

- Overstated compatibility with GPU execution and compiler optimizations: The paper asserts that the lack of implicit stacks makes NiLang programs compatible with GPU kernels and compiler optimizations (Sec. 2, 5), but this is not systematically demonstrated. The bundle adjustment GPU result is noted as a 200× speedup with "no more than 10 lines of code," but details on kernel fusion, memory coalescing, or occupancy are absent. More critically, the restriction against shared reading of variables (Sec. 6) limits parallelism and contradicts common GPU programming patterns. This suggests the compatibility is more constrained than claimed, and the performance benefits may stem from algorithmic specialization rather than general-purpose reversibility advantages.

- Lack of usability and adoption analysis for the embedded DSL: The paper does not address the practical barrier of requiring users to rewrite programs in a reversible style using a domain-specific language. Unlike mainstream AD tools that work on imperative code with minimal changes, NiLang requires careful manual construction of reversible functions, ancilla management, and control flow annotations. There is no discussion of programmer effort, debugging support, or error messages when reversibility fails. For the approach to be adopted, it must offer compelling benefits that outweigh this cognitive and implementation cost—yet the paper does not provide a cost-benefit analysis or user study to support this.

- No benchmark on standard deep learning model: The paper claims superior performance and memory efficiency for automatic differentiation but only evaluates on scientific computing tasks like GMM and bundle adjustment. To support its broader impact claim, it must demonstrate results on a standard deep learning architecture such as a convolutional neural network or multi-layer perceptron trained on a common dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10 or MNIST). Without this, readers cannot assess whether the advantages generalize beyond niche applications. The authors should add a benchmark comparing NiLang’s end-to-end training time and peak memory against JAX or PyTorch on a small CNN, using equivalent hardware and batch sizes.

- Missing numerical accuracy validation for gradients: The paper assumes floating-point operations are effectively reversible and uses this to compute gradients, but provides no empirical validation of gradient correctness. For credibility, it must compare NiLang’s computed gradients against finite differences or a trusted AD tool on a nontrivial function—such as a small neural network or a physics simulation step. A table showing relative error norms (e.g., L2 difference normalized by gradient magnitude) across several test cases would close this gap. This is especially important given the known numerical risks of uncomputing in finite precision.

- No worked example of Hessian computation: Section 4.2 claims the framework can compute Hessians by combining forward and reverse mode AD, referencing a graph embedding benchmark in 'Sec. D.2', which is absent. To substantiate this claim, the paper needs a concrete example: compute the full Hessian of a low-dimensional function (e.g., Rosenbrock or a logistic regression loss with three parameters) using NiLang, report the runtime, and compare the result to an analytical or finite-difference baseline. This would demonstrate both correctness and practicality of nested AD in the system.

- Unverified memory model in benchmarks: The paper attributes memory efficiency to reversible execution but does not measure or report actual memory usage during backpropagation in its benchmarks. For the GMM and bundle adjustment examples, it should include instrumentation showing peak memory allocation during the forward and backward passes, directly compared to a checkpointing-based AD system like Zygote under the same conditions. Without such data, the central claim of reduced memory footprint remains unsupported by evidence.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include direct comparisons with JAX, PyTorch, or Zygote on standard machine learning models (e.g., MLP, CNN) in terms of both runtime and memory usage

- Provide empirical validation of gradient and Hessian accuracy using finite-difference checks or trusted AD tools across multiple benchmarks

- Clarify the memory model by measuring peak memory during backpropagation and reconciling the theoretical complexity (Bennett's algorithm) with the observed constant memory claims

- Demonstrate GPU compatibility with detailed profiling (e.g., occupancy, bandwidth utilization) and relax or justify the restriction on shared variable reading

- Add a usability discussion addressing the programming effort required to write or convert code in NiLang, including examples of common pitfalls and debugging strategies

comments.title: Incorrect gradient transformation in reverse pass
comments.quote:
$
f ^ {- 1}: (\vec {y}, \vec {g} _ {y}) \rightarrow (\vec {x}, \vec {g} _ {y} ^ {T} \frac {\partial \vec {y}}{\partial \vec {x}}) \tag {4}
$$

It ca
comments.explanation: Equation (4) incorrectly expresses the gradient transformation in reverse mode AD. The term $\vec{g}_y^T \frac{\partial \vec{y}}{\partial \vec{x}}$ yields a row vector, but gradients must be column vectors to match the standard chain rule. The correct form should output $\left(\frac{\partial \vec{y}}{\partial \vec{x}}\right)^T \vec{g}_y$, preserving vector orientation. Without this transpose, the composition of forward and reverse mappings does not yield an identity on the gradient component, undermining the correctness of the AD formulation.

comments.title: Missing section invalidates Hessian claim
comments.quote: We will use this approach to compute Hessians in the graph embedding benchmark in Sec. D.2.
comments.explanation: The paper references 'Sec. D.2' for a graph embedding benchmark that is absent from the provided text. This omission prevents verification of the claimed Hessian computation method. Since higher-order derivatives are sensitive to implementation correctness and numerical stability, citing an unreferenced section fails to substantiate the claim. Replace this with a concrete example or remove the reference until supporting material is included.

comments.title: Floating-point reversibility lacks numerical justification
comments.quote: While for floating point numbers, none of the above operations are rigorously reversible. However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point + and - operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion.
comments.explanation: The claim that floating-point addition and subtraction can be treated as reversible by ignoring round-off errors is not justified. In IEEE 754 arithmetic, operations like (1.0 + 1e-16) - 1e-16 do not recover the original value due to rounding. This breaks reversibility and risks gradient inaccuracies, especially in deep chains. The paper cites Appendix F for safety, but its absence prevents validation. Without error bounds or empirical verification, this assumption remains a critical vulnerability in the AD correctness argument.

comments.title: Incorrect time overhead claim for reverse computing
comments.quote: It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1).
comments.explanation: The claim that reverse computing incurs no time overhead contradicts the paper's own analysis. Equation (2) and the citation to Bennett (1989) state a time complexity of $O(T^{1.585})$ for space-efficient reverse computing. Unlike checkpointing, which retrieves states in $O(T)$ via recomputation, reverse computing requires uncomputation with polynomial overhead. This misrepresentation distorts the trade-off narrative and must be corrected to reflect the actual cost of reversibility.

comments.title: Overstated memory savings for NICE network
comments.quote: It can also be used to differentiate reversible neural networks like normalizing flows *(Kobyzev et al., 2019)* to save memory, e.g. back-propagating NICE network *(Dinh et al., 2014)* with only constant space overheads.
comments.explanation: The claim of constant space overhead for backpropagating NICE networks conflicts with the paper's formal result that reverse computing has minimum space complexity $O(S \log(T/S))$. Bennett's algorithm achieves logarithmic, not constant, space. Even with algorithmic reversibility, ancilla management and parameter storage introduce non-constant overhead. The claim should be revised to reflect logarithmic scaling, which aligns with the theoretical analysis and avoids overstating the memory benefits.

comments.title: Exponentiation typo in Bennett loop index
comments.quote: bennett([s1,...,], k, i+len+k^(j-1), len+k) for j=1,2,...,k
comments.explanation: The expression `k^(j-1)` in the index calculation likely intends linear stride but uses exponentiation, which would cause exponential growth in memory offsets. For $k=3$, $j=3$, this gives $3^2=9$, creating invalid strides. The correct form should be `k*(j-1)` to reflect standard segment indexing in recursive reversal schemes. This typo, if uncorrected, invalidates the algorithm's memory access pattern and must be fixed for correctness.

comments.title: Ambiguous array notation in recursive call
comments.quote: bennett([s1,...,], k, i+len+k^(j-1), len+k) for j=1,2,...,k
comments.explanation: The argument `[s1,...,]` is syntactically malformed—missing a closing bracket and undefined element count. In a reversible recursion context, the state collection must be precisely specified. Replace this with a well-formed slice such as `s[i:i+len+k]` to ensure clarity and prevent misinterpretation of dataflow in the recursive structure.

comments.title: Fixed segment length contradicts recursive uncomputation
comments.quote: bennett([s1,...,], k, i+len+k^(j-1), len+k) for j=1,2,...,k
comments.explanation: The loop passes a constant `len+k` as segment length across all $k$ recursive calls, but Bennett's algorithm requires segment sizes to decrease with recursion depth to achieve space efficiency. Holding length constant prevents the recursive subdivision needed for logarithmic space. Adjust the length parameter to scale with recursion level (e.g., `len // k`) to align with the algorithm's design and complexity claims.

comments.title: Incorrect reversal of while loop control flow
comments.quote: while postcondition  $\sim$  (loop body) assert precondition  $= =$  true
comments.explanation: The backward while loop uses `while postcondition`, but correct reversal requires running while the precondition is not yet restored—i.e., `while not precondition`. Using `postcondition` as the loop condition fails to mirror the forward logic, especially since `postcondition` may not reflect the state needed to invert the loop. This breaks reversibility. Revise the control flow to ensure the exit condition in reverse matches the entry condition in forward execution.

comments.title: Assertion breaks reversibility in loop initialization
comments.quote: assert postcondition  $= =$  false
comments.explanation: Using an assertion to enforce `postcondition == false` before loop execution violates reversibility, as assertions are not invertible operations. The backward pass cannot restore the pre-assertion state. Instead, a reversible assignment (e.g., `postcondition := false`) should initialize the variable, ensuring it can be undone. Replace the assertion with a reversible operation to maintain invertibility throughout the loop.

comments.title: Hessian computation overstates efficiency
comments.quote: By wrapping the elementary type with Dual defined in package ForwardDiff and throwing it into the gradient program defined in NiLang, one obtains one row/column of the Hessian matrix.
comments.explanation: The method described computes Hessian-vector products, not full rows or columns, unless combined with basis probing. A single execution with Dual numbers yields only one directional derivative. The claim that a full row/column is obtained 'by throwing' into the gradient program overstates the efficiency and simplicity. Clarify that multiple evaluations are needed for full Hessian extraction, aligning the description with standard nested AD practices.

comments.title: Unfair exclusion of mainstream AD frameworks
comments.quote: Since most tensor based AD software like famous TensorFlow and PyTorch are not designed for the using cases used in our benchmarks, we do not include those package to avoid an unfair comparison.
comments.explanation: The justification for excluding PyTorch and TensorFlow is circular: the benchmarks (GMM, bundle adjustment) are standard optimization problems routinely handled by these frameworks. Claiming they are 'not designed for the use cases' avoids direct comparison where NiLang might show no advantage. Either include comparisons on equivalent tasks or provide a technical reason (e.g., lack of in-place support) that justifies exclusion based on implementation constraints.

comments.title: Treeverse logarithmic overhead claim lacks derivation
comments.quote: By carefully choosing either a  $t$  or  $d$ , the overhead in time and space can be both logarithmic.
comments.explanation: Equation (1) gives $T_c = tT$ and $S_c = dS$, with $T = \eta(t,d)$, but no definition of $\eta(t,d)$ is provided. Achieving logarithmic time overhead would require $t = O(\log T)$, implying $T_c = O(T \log T)$, not $O(\log T)$. The claim that both overheads can be logarithmic is unsupported. Revise to state that space can be logarithmic at the cost of $O(T \log T)$ time, reflecting the actual trade-off.

comments.title: Energy efficiency argument rests on flawed model
comments.quote: In the time-space trade-off scheme in Sec. 2, we show the time to uncompute a unit of memory is exponential to $n$ as $Q_{n}=(2k-1)^{n}$, and the computing energy also increases exponentially.
comments.explanation: The expression $Q_n = (2k-1)^n$ does not appear in Section 2 and contradicts standard reversible computing results, where uncomputation time is $O(n)$ via Bennett's method. The exponential model leads to an incorrect energy comparison. Replace this with the correct polynomial-time analysis and re-evaluate the energy trade-off based on actual reversible computation costs.

comments.title: Ambiguous macro expansion in compute-copy-uncompute
comments.quote: @routine begin n  $\leftarrow$  zero(T) n  $+ =$  abs(x) end y!.re  $+ =$  log(n) y!.im  $+ =$  angle(x) -@routine end
comments.explanation: The text claims the bottom two lines uncompute the top two, but only the allocation and `abs(x)` are inside `@routine`. The operations `log(n)` and `angle(x)` modify `y!` and are not reversed by `~@routine`. This creates confusion about what is being uncomputed. Clarify that `@routine` only manages ancilla uncomputation and does not reverse side effects on output variables.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces NiLang, a reversible embedded DSL for automatic differentiation that aims to eliminate memory overhead from checkpointing. While the approach is innovative, the evaluation is insufficient for a top venue, with limited benchmarks and inadequate comparison to state-of-the-art methods. The theoretical analysis contains questionable assumptions about floating-point reversibility, and the practical limitations are not adequately addressed.

The paper presents an interesting approach to reducing memory overhead in automatic differentiation through reversible programming. The language design of NiLang appears comprehensive, with support for arrays, complex numbers, and control flows. The implementation of CUDA kernel compatibility is a notable strength. However, the experimental evaluation is too narrow and the theoretical foundations contain significant gaps that undermine the claims.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Benchmarking Against Modern Checkpointing Techniques: The paper only benchmarks against Tapenade, Zygote, ForwardDiff, and ReverseDiff, but excludes modern checkpointing techniques like gradient checkpointing (Chen et al., 2016) or subgraph ad methods that are directly relevant to the memory-accuracy trade-offs the paper addresses. The GMM benchmark shows NiLang performing similarly to Tapenade in gradient computation speed, which fails to demonstrate the claimed advantages. For a paper claiming 'state-of-the-art performance', comprehensive benchmarking against recent memory-efficient AD methods is essential, particularly on large-scale models where memory overhead is critical.

- Questionable Treatment of Floating-Point Reversibility: Section 3.1 acknowledges that floating-point operations are not rigorously reversible due to round-off errors, but then states 'we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point + and - operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers'. This is a significant assumption that undermines gradient accuracy. The paper claims this is 'safe in most cases' but provides no empirical evidence of gradient accuracy degradation. For an AD system, numerical stability is paramount, and this cavalier treatment of floating-point arithmetic could lead to incorrect gradients in practice.

- Missing Analysis of Computational Overhead: The paper focuses on memory savings but provides insufficient analysis of the computational overhead introduced by reversible programming. The GMM benchmark shows the objective function is '~2x slower' due to uncomputing overhead, but this is not systematically analyzed across different problem types. The theoretical analysis in Section 2 suggests reverse computing has worse time complexity than optimal checkpointing, yet the paper dismisses this as 'worst case' without characterizing when the practical case differs. Readers need to understand the time-space trade-offs to evaluate whether the memory savings justify the computational costs.

- Inadequate Discussion of Integration Challenges: Section 6 briefly mentions challenges with BLAS operations and shared-reading restrictions on GPU, but these are fundamental limitations that deserve deeper analysis. The paper claims compatibility with existing Julia ecosystem tools but provides no evidence or examples of integration with packages like Flux.jl or Zygote.jl. The restriction on shared-reading in GPU kernels severely limits the approach's applicability to common deep learning operations like batch normalization or attention mechanisms. These limitations should be quantified and their impact on practical usability assessed.

- Limited Scope of Experimental Evaluation: The benchmarks only cover GMM and bundle adjustment, plus brief mentions of sparse matrix operations and graph embedding in appendices. For a paper claiming broad applicability to 'differentiate everything', this is insufficient. There are no experiments with deep neural networks, scientific computing PDE solvers, or other domains where memory overhead is a known bottleneck. The GPU benchmark only shows bundle adjustment, which is not representative of typical ML workloads. A more comprehensive evaluation across diverse computational patterns is needed to substantiate the universal applicability claims.

- Missing demonstration of gradient accuracy: The paper acknowledges that floating-point operations are not rigorously reversible due to round-off errors, but provides no empirical validation that gradients computed via NiLang are numerically accurate. For an AD system, gradient correctness is fundamental. The paper should include a benchmark comparing NiLang's gradients against reference implementations (e.g., finite differences or high-precision arithmetic) on problems where floating-point cancellation occurs. A standard test would be differentiating the log-sum-exp function or ill-conditioned matrix operations to quantify gradient errors.

- No worked example of reversible transformation: The paper claims any program can be converted to reversible form but never demonstrates this process for a complete, non-trivial algorithm. Readers need to see how the transformation works in practice, including the overhead introduced. The paper should walk through converting a standard algorithm like LU decomposition or a neural network layer to reversible NiLang code, showing both the forward and backward passes and quantifying the time/space overhead. This would help evaluate the practicality of their approach.

- Missing comparison with gradient checkpointing: The paper benchmarks against traditional AD tools but excludes modern gradient checkpointing methods like Chen et al. (2016) or activation checkpointing in PyTorch, which directly address the memory overhead problem. Since the paper claims 'state-of-the-art performance' in memory efficiency, it must compare against these methods. The benchmark should include large models where checkpointing is typically used (e.g., ResNets or Transformers) to demonstrate NiLang's advantages or limitations.

- No guidance on reversible programming patterns: The paper introduces reversible programming concepts but provides minimal guidance on how to write efficient reversible code. Section 3.2 shows basic examples but lacks patterns for common ML operations. The paper should include a 'best practices' section with reversible implementations of key operations like matrix factorizations, convolutions, or attention mechanisms, explaining the design choices and their impact on performance. Without this, users cannot effectively apply the framework to real problems.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive benchmarking against modern gradient checkpointing methods (e.g., Chen et al., 2016) on large-scale models where memory overhead is critical

- Provide empirical analysis of gradient accuracy when treating floating-point operations as reversible, including error bounds and failure cases

- Systematically analyze and report computational overhead across diverse problem types, with clear characterization of when reversible computing is beneficial

- Demonstrate practical integration with existing Julia ML ecosystem packages and quantify the impact of GPU shared-reading restrictions on common operations

- Expand experimental evaluation to include deep neural networks, scientific computing applications, and other domains beyond the two narrow benchmarks shown

comments.title: Incorrect Jacobian identity claim
comments.quote: It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering  $\frac{\partial\vec{y}}{\partial\vec{x}}\frac{\partial\vec{x}}{\partial\vec{y}} = \mathbb{1}$ .
comments.explanation: The Jacobian identity $\frac{\partial\vec{y}}{\partial\vec{x}}\frac{\partial\vec{x}}{\partial\vec{y}} = \mathbb{1}$ only holds when the function is locally invertible and input/output dimensions match. For general functions, Jacobians can be rectangular and their product is not the identity. Rewrite the claim as 'This holds when the function is locally invertible and the input and output have the same dimension, where $\frac{\partial\vec{y}}{\partial\vec{x}}\frac{\partial\vec{x}}{\partial\vec{y}} = \mathbb{1}$'.

comments.title: Inconsistent gradient notation in equations
comments.explanation: The gradient notation is inconsistent. In equation (3), the output gradient should be $\vec{g}_x^T \frac{\partial \vec{y}}{\partial \vec{x}}$ not $\vec{g}_y^T \frac{\partial \vec{x}}{\partial \vec{y}}$. In equation (4), the output should be $\vec{g}_y^T \frac{\partial \vec{x}}{\partial \vec{y}}$ not $\vec{g}_y^T \frac{\partial \vec{y}}{\partial \vec{x}}$. Rewrite equation (3) as $f: (\vec {x}, \vec {g} _ {x}) \rightarrow (\vec {y}, \vec {g} _ {x} ^ {T} \frac {\partial \vec {y}}{\partial \vec {x}})$ and equation (4) as $f ^ {- 1}: (\vec {y}, \vec {g} _ {y}) \rightarrow (\vec {x}, \vec {g} _ {y} ^ {T} \frac {\partial \vec{x}}{\partial \vec{y}})$.

comments.title: Contradiction in checkpointing space overhead claim
comments.quote: It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1). The checkpointing scheme snapshots the output in every step, and the reverse computing scheme allocates extra storage for storing outputs in every step.
comments.explanation: The paper contradicts itself. Earlier it describes checkpointing as storing snapshots at selected time steps S = {s_a, s_b, ...}, but then claims it 'snapshots the output in every step' with linear space overhead. Selective checkpointing has space complexity O(|S|), not O(T). The linear space overhead only applies to naive checkpointing at every step, which is not the optimal approach described. Rewrite the sentence to clarify: 'The naive checkpointing scheme that snapshots every step suffers from linear space overhead, while selective checkpointing at steps S = {s_a, s_b, ...} has space complexity O(|S|).'

comments.title: Incorrect time overhead claim for checkpointing
comments.quote: It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1).
comments.explanation: The claim that checkpointing traces back states 'without time overhead' is incorrect. When retrieving a state s_k not in S, the algorithm must 're-compute s_k from s_j' where s_j is the nearest checkpoint. This recomputation introduces time overhead. The paper later states that checkpointing can achieve zero space overhead 'with a time complexity O(T^2)' by recomputing from the beginning, which contradicts the 'no time overhead' claim. Remove 'without time overhead' as both methods have time-space tradeoffs.

comments.title: Undefined function in treeverse complexity equation
comments.quote: where  $T = \eta(t, d)$  holds.
comments.explanation: Equation (1) references an undefined function η(t,d) with the statement 'where T = η(t,d) holds'. This function is not defined in the paper, making the complexity analysis incomplete. The relationship between t, d, and T needs to be specified for the overhead analysis to be meaningful. Add the definition of η(t,d) or remove the reference to it.

comments.title: Unjustified invertibility assumption in reversible transformation
comments.quote: Without prior knowledge, a regular program can be transpiled to the reversible style is by doing the transformation in Listing. 1.
comments.explanation: The paper assumes that any regular program can be made reversible by simply changing += to -= operations, but this requires that each function f_i be invertible. The transformation in Listing 1 to Listing 2 only works if f_i has a well-defined inverse. This assumption is not justified and may not hold for general computations. Add a statement clarifying that this transformation requires invertible functions or explain how non-invertible operations are handled.

comments.title: Memory usage formula inconsistency
comments.quote:
The analytical expression of memory usage in unit of floating point number is

$$S = (2+d^2)k + 2d + P, \eqno(5)$$
$$S_r = (3+d^2+d)k + 2\log_2 k + P, \eqno(6)$$ 

where $d$ and $k$ are the size and number of covariance matrices. $P=\frac{d(d+1)}{2}k+k+dk$ is the size of parameter space.
comments.explanation: The memory usage formulas appear inconsistent with the definition of P. Substituting P = d(d+1)/2 * k + k + dk into equation (5) gives S = (2+d^2)k + 2d + d(d+1)/2 * k + k + dk. This simplifies to S = (3 + d^2 + d(d+1)/2 + d)k + 2d. However, the paper claims S represents the original program memory, which should be simpler than the reversible version S_r. Yet S_r = (3+d^2+d)k + 2log_2 k + P appears to have fewer terms involving d^2 and d than the expanded S. This suggests either the formulas are incorrect or P is defined incorrectly. The authors should verify these derivations and ensure consistency between the analytical expressions and the claimed memory relationships.

comments.title: ForwardDiff memory estimation contradiction
comments.quote: The ForwardDiff memory usage is approximately the original size times the batch size, where the batch size is 12 by default.
comments.explanation: This statement about ForwardDiff memory usage appears incorrect. ForwardDiff uses forward-mode automatic differentiation with dual numbers, which typically requires memory proportional to the number of input dimensions being differentiated simultaneously, not a 'batch size'. ForwardDiff doesn't have a concept of 'batch size' in the same way reverse-mode AD does. The memory usage should scale with the dimensionality of the input being differentiated, not a fixed batch size of 12. This suggests a misunderstanding of how ForwardDiff works internally. The authors should either correct this description or provide a citation for this batch size claim.

comments.title: Incorrect XOR notation in reversible transformation
comments.quote: Mathematically, any irreversible mapping  $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{f}(\arg s\ldots)$  can be trivially transformed to its reversible form  $\mathbf{y} += \mathbf{f}(\arg s\ldots)$  or  $\mathbf{y} \not\supseteq f(\arg s\ldots)$  ( $\not\supseteq$  is the bit-wise XOR), where  $\mathbf{y}$  is a pre-emptied variable.

comments.title: Logarithmic number system reversibility requires non-zero condition
comments.quote: For logarithmic number system and tropical number system (Speyer & Sturmfels, 2009),  $y *= f(args...)$  and  $y /= f(args...)$  as reversible (not introducing the zero element).
comments.explanation:
The claim that multiplication and division are reversible in logarithmic number systems is incomplete. In logarithmic number systems, while multiplication becomes addition in the log domain, division by zero or multiplication by zero remain problematic. The parenthetical '(not introducing the zero element)' suggests awareness, but the statement needs the explicit condition that $f(args...) \neq 0$ for reversibility.

Add the condition 'provided $f(args...) \neq 0$' after the statement to clarify this restriction.

comments.title: Table notation error in checkpointing complexity
comments.quote: |  Checkpointing | O(T)/O(T+S) | O(T2)/O(S)  |
comments.explanation: The table entry 'O(T2)' contains a notation error - the '2' should be an exponent to represent O(T^2), which matches the text stating checkpointing can achieve O(T^2) time complexity for space efficiency. Rewrite 'O(T2)' as 'O(T^2)' in the table.

comments.title: Inconsistent reverse function notation
comments.quote: Macro @i generates two functions that are reversible to each other, multiplier and ~multiplier, each defines a mapping  $\mathbb{R}^3\to \mathbb{R}^3$ .
comments.explanation:
The text describes the reverse function as '~multiplier' but the code example uses '(-multiplier)'. This inconsistency creates confusion about the actual syntax. Since the code shows '(-multiplier)' is the working syntax, the text should match.

Change '~multiplier' to '(-multiplier)' in the text to match the code example.

comments.title: Undefined 'pre-emptied variable' concept
comments.quote: Mathematically, any irreversible mapping  $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{f}(\arg s\ldots)$  can be trivially transformed to its reversible form  $\mathbf{y} += \mathbf{f}(\arg s\ldots)$  or  $\mathbf{y} \not\supseteq f(\arg s\ldots)$  ( $\not\supseteq$  is the bit-wise XOR), where  $\mathbf{y}$  is a pre-emptied variable.
comments.explanation:
The term 'pre-emptied variable' is not defined in the text. This concept is crucial for the reversible transformation to work, as the variable must be initialized to a known state (typically zero) before the operation. Without this definition, readers cannot understand the requirements for the transformation.

Add a definition: 'where a pre-emptied variable means a variable initialized to zero (or another known constant value) before the reversible operation' after the first sentence.

comments.title: Missing justification for floating-point reversibility assumption
comments.quote: However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point + and - operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion. In Appendix F, we will show doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation.
comments.explanation:
The paper makes a significant assumption about ignoring floating-point round-off errors without providing immediate justification. While Appendix F is referenced, readers need at least a brief explanation of why this assumption is reasonable or what conditions make it 'safe in most cases' to evaluate the claims in this section.

Add a brief justification: 'This assumption is valid because round-off errors in floating-point addition and subtraction are bounded and typically small compared to the values being operated on, and they can be controlled through careful implementation choices such as using extended precision when available' before referencing Appendix F.

comments.title: Macro name inconsistency in text vs code
comments.quote: Compute-copy-uncompute is a useful design pattern in reversible programming so that we created a pair of macros @routine and  $\sim$  @routine for it. One can rewrite the above function as in Listing. 6.
comments.explanation: The text describes the macros as '@routine' and '~@routine', but Listing 6 uses '-@routine' for the closing macro. This inconsistency creates confusion about the correct syntax. Rewrite the text as 'Compute-copy-uncompute is a useful design pattern in reversible programming so that we created a pair of macros @routine and -@routine for it. One can rewrite the above function as in Listing 6.' because the code example shows '-@routine' as the actual syntax used.

comments.title: Undefined function name syntax in code listings
comments.quote: @i @inline function  $(: = =)$  (log)(y!::Complex{T}, x::Complex{T}) where T
comments.explanation: The function definition syntax '$(: = =)$ (log)' appears to be a LaTeX rendering artifact and is not valid Julia syntax. This makes the code examples non-executable and confusing. Rewrite the function definition as '@i @inline function log(y!::Complex{T}, x::Complex{T}) where T' because this is the standard Julia syntax for defining a function named 'log' with the given parameters.

comments.title: GPU speedup claim lacks baseline
comments.quote: In the bundle adjustment benchmark, NiLang performs the best on CPU. We also compiled our adjoint program to GPU with no more than 10 lines of code with KernelAbstractions, which provides another $\sim$200$\times$ speed up.
comments.explanation: The 200× GPU speedup claim is incomplete because it doesn't specify what this speedup is relative to. Is it 200× faster than the CPU version of NiLang, or 200× faster than the CPU version of the best competing method? The sentence structure suggests it's comparing GPU to CPU performance of the same NiLang code, but this needs to be explicit. Without a clear baseline, readers cannot interpret the significance of this speedup. The authors should clarify: 'which provides ~200× speedup compared to the CPU version' or specify the exact baseline reference.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about 10-line overhead
comments.quote: We will differentiate CUDA kernels with no more than extra 10 lines in the bundle adjustment benchmark in Sec. 5.
comments.explanation: This specific quantitative claim about requiring 'no more than extra 10 lines' to differentiate CUDA kernels needs verification. The paper should either provide the exact code comparison showing the 10-line difference in Section 5, or remove this specific numerical claim if it cannot be substantiated. Such precise claims about implementation overhead require concrete evidence.

comments.title: Grammatical error in Julia speed description
comments.quote: Julia is a language with high abstraction, however, its clever design of type inference and just in time compiling make it has a C like speed.
comments.explanation: The sentence contains a grammatical error where 'make it has' should be 'make it have' or 'makes it have'. The subject 'design' is singular, so it should be 'makes it have'. Rewrite this sentence as 'Julia is a language with high abstraction, however, its clever design of type inference and just in time compiling makes it have C-like speed.' because the current phrasing is grammatically incorrect.

comments.title: Inconsistent capitalization of 'C-like'
comments.quote: make it has a C like speed
comments.explanation: The phrase 'C like speed' should be hyphenated as 'C-like speed' for proper compound adjective formatting. This appears to be a typographical error that affects readability. Rewrite 'a C like speed' as 'C-like speed' because compound adjectives before nouns typically require hyphens.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents NiLang, a reversible embedded domain-specific language in Julia for automatic differentiation. The core idea is to use reverse computing instead of checkpointing to trace back intermediate states, enabling GPU-compatible AD without implicit stack operations. The paper claims state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks including GMM and bundle adjustment.

The paper tackles a genuine problem in automatic differentiation—memory overhead from checkpointing—and proposes a clever alternative using reversible programming. The implementation in Julia is technically impressive and the benchmarks show competitive performance. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues: the theoretical comparison between checkpointing and reverse computing is flawed, the evaluation does not isolate the contribution of reversibility from other optimizations, and the claimed advantages for GPU compatibility are not rigorously demonstrated.

Overview issues:

- Flawed theoretical comparison between checkpointing and reverse computing: Section 2 presents a complexity comparison that is misleading. Table 1 claims that reverse computing has O(T)/O(T+S) time/space for the most time-efficient case, but this is only true if the program is already reversible. For general irreversible programs, making them reversible introduces overhead that is not accounted for. The comparison also uses different baselines: the checkpointing analysis assumes the original irreversible program, while the reverse computing analysis implicitly assumes the program is written reversibly from scratch. The Bennett's algorithm complexity in equation (2) is presented without derivation and appears to contain undefined variables (t, |S|). The claim that 'reverse computing does not show advantage from the above complexity analysis' is then contradicted by listing five advantages that are not quantified. This section needs a rigorous, apples-to-apples comparison that accounts for the cost of making programs reversible.

- Evaluation does not isolate the contribution of reversibility: The benchmarks in Section 5 compare NiLang against Tapenade, Zygote, ForwardDiff, and ReverseDiff. However, NiLang's performance advantage could come from multiple factors: the reversible execution model, Julia's JIT compilation, custom memory management, or CUDA kernel optimizations. The paper does not include ablation studies to separate these effects. For example, the bundle adjustment benchmark shows NiLang outperforming Tapenade on CPU, but it's unclear whether this is due to reversibility or simply because Julia is faster than the Tapenade-generated Fortran/C code. The paper should include a baseline where the same algorithm is implemented in Julia without reversible constructs, or compare NiLang against a checkpointing-based AD in Julia (e.g., Zygote) on the same benchmarks. Without such controls, the claimed advantages of reverse computing over checkpointing are not empirically supported.

- Missing analysis of memory vs. compute trade-offs for deep networks: The paper claims that reverse computing reduces memory footprint, but the only memory analysis is for the GMM benchmark (Figure 4), which shows a modest reduction. There is no evaluation on deep neural networks with many layers, where checkpointing is typically used. The paper mentions that NiLang can differentiate reversible neural networks with 'constant space overheads,' but provides no experiments to demonstrate this. For long sequences or deep networks, the time overhead of Bennett's algorithm (O(T^{1.585}) in the space-efficient case) could be prohibitive. The paper should include benchmarks on models like ResNets or Transformers, comparing peak memory and runtime against standard checkpointing strategies (e.g., the treeverse algorithm or periodic checkpointing). Without this, the practical relevance of the approach for deep learning remains unsubstantiated.

- Unsupported claims about GPU kernel compatibility: Section 4.3 claims that NiLang can differentiate CUDA kernels with 'no more than extra 10 lines' and that this provides a '~200x speed up' in the bundle adjustment benchmark. However, the paper does not explain how reversible control flow (which relies on assertions and branch keepers) is compiled to GPU kernels that cannot handle exceptions. The @invcheckoff macro is mentioned as a way to remove checks, but its correctness is not analyzed. The shared-reading restriction mentioned in Section 6 ('the shared-reading of a variable is not allowed') is a severe limitation for GPU parallelism that is not discussed in the evaluation. The paper should provide a detailed explanation of how reversible constructs are mapped to GPU kernels, including how memory management and control flow are handled, and should benchmark the GPU implementation against established GPU AD frameworks (e.g., PyTorch, JAX) to validate the claimed speedup.

- Inadequate handling of floating-point reversibility and numerical stability: Section 3.1 acknowledges that floating-point operations are not rigorously reversible due to round-off errors, but states that 'doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation.' This is a critical issue that is not adequately addressed. If forward and backward passes do not exactly reverse each other due to floating-point non-associativity or rounding, the gradient computation could be incorrect or unstable. The paper provides no analysis of numerical errors, no experiments on ill-conditioned problems, and no guidelines for when the approximation breaks down. Appendix F is referenced but not included in the provided content. The paper should include a numerical analysis of reversibility errors, demonstrate that gradients are accurate to machine precision on standard test problems, and discuss conditions under which the approach may fail (e.g., catastrophic cancellation, denormalized numbers).

- Missing discussion of limitations and scope: Section 6 briefly mentions three challenges but does not discuss several important limitations. First, the approach requires users to write programs in a reversible style, which is a significant cognitive burden and may not be feasible for large, existing codebases. Second, the paper does not discuss how stochastic operations (e.g., dropout, random sampling) are handled—these are inherently irreversible and would require special treatment. Third, the claim that 'any program can be written in a reversible style' is true in theory but the overhead of converting arbitrary code (as shown in Listing 1) is O(T) in space, which negates the memory advantage. The paper should clearly state the conditions under which reverse computing is beneficial (e.g., programs with many reversible operations, or where the user is willing to restructure code) and when it is not (e.g., programs with heavy use of random numbers or in-place updates).

- No concrete reversible DGP example with memory savings: The paper claims that reverse computing reduces memory, but the only memory analysis is for the GMM benchmark, which shows a modest reduction. There is no worked example of a specific data-generating process (DGP) or model where the reversible DSL demonstrably saves memory compared to checkpointing. For instance, the paper could compute equation (5) and (6) for a concrete reversible neural network like a NICE flow or a unitary RNN, and verify that the reversible program uses less peak memory than a standard checkpointing scheme. Without such an example, the core claim that reverse computing provides a practical memory advantage remains unsubstantiated.

- Missing ablation study isolating reversibility from Julia/JIT: The benchmarks compare NiLang against Tapenade, Zygote, and others, but NiLang's performance could be due to Julia's JIT compilation or custom memory management rather than reversibility. The paper should include a baseline where the same algorithm is implemented in Julia using standard (irreversible) constructs and checkpointing-based AD (e.g., Zygote) on the same benchmarks. For example, in the bundle adjustment benchmark, compare NiLang's CPU time against a Julia implementation that uses Zygote for gradients. Without this control, the claimed advantages of reverse computing over checkpointing are not empirically supported.

- No evaluation on deep networks or long sequences: The paper claims that NiLang can differentiate reversible neural networks with 'constant space overheads,' but provides no experiments on models like ResNets or Transformers where checkpointing is typically used. The time overhead of Bennett's algorithm (O(T^{1.585}) in the space-efficient case) could be prohibitive for deep networks. The paper should include benchmarks on a standard deep learning model (e.g., a ResNet-50 or a Transformer with 12 layers) comparing peak memory and runtime against periodic checkpointing or the treeverse algorithm. Without this, the practical relevance for deep learning remains unsubstantiated.

- No guidance on handling stochastic or irreversible operations: The paper acknowledges that floating-point operations are not rigorously reversible but does not discuss how stochastic operations (e.g., dropout, random sampling) are handled. These are inherently irreversible and would require special treatment, such as storing random seeds or using a separate random number generator state. The paper should provide a concrete example of how to implement a stochastic layer (e.g., dropout) in NiLang, and discuss the memory and correctness implications. Without this, the claim that the DSL is 'general-purpose' for differentiable programming is overstated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix the theoretical comparison in Section 2: provide a clear, apples-to-apples complexity analysis that accounts for the cost of making irreversible programs reversible, and correct any undefined or erroneous variables in the equations.

- Add ablation studies that isolate the effect of reversibility from other optimizations (Julia JIT, custom memory management, CUDA kernels). Compare NiLang against a checkpointing-based AD in Julia (e.g., Zygote) on the same benchmarks.

- Include benchmarks on deep neural networks (e.g., ResNet, Transformer) that compare memory usage and runtime against standard checkpointing strategies. Demonstrate the claimed constant-space backpropagation for reversible architectures.

- Provide a detailed analysis of floating-point reversibility: quantify numerical errors on standard test problems, discuss conditions where reversibility breaks, and provide guidelines for safe usage.

- Expand the discussion of limitations to cover: the cognitive burden of reversible programming, handling of stochastic operations, and the conditions under which reverse computing is beneficial compared to checkpointing.

comments.title: Equation (3) uses inverse Jacobian incorrectly
comments.quote: f: (\vec {x}, \vec {g} _ {x}) \rightarrow (\vec {y}, \vec {g} _ {y} ^ {T} \frac {\partial \vec {x}}{\partial \vec {y}}) \tag {3}
comments.explanation: Equation (3) defines the forward mapping as updating the output gradient with g_y^T * (∂x/∂y). In standard reverse-mode AD, the adjoint of y (denoted g_y) is propagated backward via g_x = g_y^T * (∂y/∂x). The paper's notation appears to use g_x and g_y as adjoints of x and y respectively. Under that interpretation, the correct forward mapping should be (x, g_y) → (y, g_y^T * (∂y/∂x)). The paper writes ∂x/∂y, which is the inverse Jacobian of f. This is only defined when f is bijective and its Jacobian is invertible, a condition not stated or generally satisfied. The identity claimed below also relies on this inverse relationship. Rewrite equation (3) as f: (x, g_y) → (y, g_y^T * (∂y/∂x)) to match standard adjoint propagation.

comments.title: Identity claim assumes bijectivity without justification
comments.quote: It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering  $\frac{\partial\vec{y}}{\partial\vec{x}}\frac{\partial\vec{x}}{\partial\vec{y}} = \mathbb{1}$ .
comments.explanation: The paper claims that composing the forward mapping (3) and the reverse mapping (4) yields an identity because ∂y/∂x * ∂x/∂y = I. This identity holds only if f is a bijection with invertible Jacobian (i.e., f is a diffeomorphism). The paper does not state that f is bijective; in fact, the DSL is intended for general differentiable programs, which may include non-invertible operations (e.g., matrix multiplication with non-square matrices, activation functions like ReLU that are not injective). For a general differentiable function, ∂y/∂x and ∂x/∂y are not necessarily inverses — indeed, ∂x/∂y may not even be defined if f is not locally invertible. The claim is therefore false without additional assumptions. The paper should either restrict the claim to reversible (bijective) primitives or remove the identity claim and explain how the gradient mappings compose correctly for general functions.

comments.title: Table 1 space complexity for checkpointing is inconsistent
comments.quote:
|  Method | most time efficient (Time/Space) | most space efficient (Time/Space)  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  Checkpointing | $O(T)/O(T+S)$ | $O(T^2)/O(S)$  |
|  Reverse computing | $O(T)/O(T+S)$ | $O(T^{1.585})/O(S \log(T/S))$  |
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists the most time-efficient checkpointing as O(T)/O(T+S). However, the most time-efficient checkpointing (no checkpointing, recompute from scratch) has time O(T^2) and space O(S). The entry O(T)/O(T+S) corresponds to checkpointing every step, which is the most space-inefficient. The table appears to have swapped the columns: the 'most time efficient' column actually shows the most space-efficient (or vice versa). Correct the table so that the 'most time efficient' column shows O(T)/O(T+S) for both methods (which is correct for reverse computing but not for checkpointing), and the 'most space efficient' column shows O(T^2)/O(S) for checkpointing and O(T^{1.585})/O(S log(T/S)) for reverse computing. Alternatively, swap the column headers to match the entries.

comments.title: Undefined variables in equation (2)
comments.quote: T _ {r} = T \left(\frac {T}{S}\right) ^ {\frac {\ln (2 - t \cdot | S |)}{2 t + 1}}, S _ {r} = \frac {k - 1}{\ln k} S \log \frac {T}{S}. \tag {2}
comments.explanation: Equation (2) introduces variables 't' and '|S|' that are not defined in the surrounding text. The variable 't' appears in the exponent but has no prior definition; it may be intended as a parameter of the Bennett algorithm, but it is never explained. Similarly, '|S|' is ambiguous: S is already used for the per-step memory size, so |S| could mean the cardinality of a set of checkpoints, but this is not defined. The paper should define all symbols in equation (2) or remove it if it is not essential to the argument. Without definitions, the equation is unverifiable and should be corrected. Rewrite equation (2) with all symbols defined, or remove it and replace with a clear statement of the known complexity bounds.

comments.title: Missing definition of η(t,d) in equation (1)
comments.quote: where  $T = \eta(t, d)$  holds.
